title: Answering History DBQs Without Getting Overwhelmed
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Document-Based Questions are the hardest part of history class. They give you 5-10 primary source documents. You have to read them all, analyze them, and write an essay in one class period. It feels impossible. Here's how to crack the code.

The 15-Minute Game Plan

For a standard 45-minute DBQ, spend the first 15 minutes on prep, the next 25 minutes writing, and the last 5 minutes revising. Most students spend too long reading and not enough writing, then panic at the end.

Set a timer for each section. When the timer goes off, move on. A finished essay is better than a perfect introduction with no conclusion.

Step 1: Read the Question First (2 Minutes)

Before you look at any documents, read the prompt. What are they actually asking? Circle the task words: 'analyze,' 'compare,' 'evaluate,' 'explain.' This tells you what kind of argument you need to make.

Also note the time period and topic. You'll be looking for evidence related to this in the documents.

Step 2: Scan the Documents for Source Information (5 Minutes)

For each document, look at who wrote it, when, and to whom. A letter from a soldier in 1863 is different from a textbook written in 1990. The source's perspective is as important as the content.

Write a quick code for each document: 'POV' (point of view), 'evidence,' 'counterargument,' 'foreign' (outside knowledge needed). This will help you group them later.

Step 3: Group the Documents (8 Minutes)

Most DBQs give you more documents than you need. You don't have to use all of them. Look for 3-4 documents that support one argument, and 2-3 that support another. That's your body paragraph structure.

Write a quick outline on scratch paper:
• Thesis: Your main argument
• Body 1: Point A + Docs 1, 3, 5
• Body 2: Point B + Docs 2, 4, 7
• Body 3: Counterargument + Docs 6, 8

This outline is your roadmap. Now the essay writes itself.

Step 4: Write a Simple Thesis (3 Minutes)

A good thesis answers the prompt and lists your points. Use this formula: 'Although [counterargument], [your argument] because [point 1], [point 2], and [point 3].'

Example: 'Although the Industrial Revolution caused harsh working conditions, it ultimately improved living standards because it created jobs, lowered prices, and spurred technological innovation.'

See how the three points are right there? Those are your body paragraphs.

Step 5: Write Body Paragraphs (20 Minutes)

Each body paragraph needs:
• A topic sentence (one of your three points)
• Evidence from at least one document (quote or paraphrase)
• Analysis (why this evidence proves your point)
• A second document or outside knowledge for depth

The formula: Point → Evidence → Analysis → Link to thesis. Repeat for each paragraph.

Step 6: Write a Quick Conclusion (5 Minutes)

Don't introduce new evidence. Restate your thesis in different words. Summarize your three points. End with a broader thought — how does this topic connect to a later historical event or today?

A weak conclusion is better than no conclusion. If you're out of time, write two sentences and move on.

Outside Knowledge (The Hidden Points)

DBQ rubrics reward you for bringing in information not in the documents. Before the exam, review major events, people, and terms for each time period. Even one relevant outside example can boost your score by a full point.

Write 'OK' in the margin next to the paragraph where you add outside knowledge. This reminds the grader to look for it.

DBQs are learnable. Practice with old prompts from your textbook or online. The more you do, the faster you'll get. Speed comes from pattern recognition, not panic.
